revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 若宮八幡社 |画像 = |所在地 = [[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町10-28 |位置 = |祭神 = 帯中津比古命（[[仲哀天皇]]）
品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）
大雀命 |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''若宮八幡社'''（わかみやはちまんしゃ）は、[[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町に鎮座する[[神社]]である。 == 概要 == 大分市中心部の南部、[[上野丘陵]]の麓に位置しており。西側で[[大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校]]に、北側で[[久大本線]]の線路に接する。 社伝では、[[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]）に[[大友氏]]初代当主[[大友能直]]が[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[豊後国|豊後]]二国の[[守護]]として下向した際、[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請して建立したと伝えられるが、史実は不明である。当初は古河津留（現在の岩田町周辺）に建立されたが、[[大分川]]に近くしばしば水害に遭ったため、現在地近くに遷座した。中世の[[大友氏遺跡|大友氏館]]から見て南西（[[裏鬼門]]）に位置しており、[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]の鎮守として大友氏から代々崇敬を受けた{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf 大分今昔 第十一章 若松通り]}} 渡辺克己。 明治時代に入ると、明治14年（[[1881年]]）に南新地（現在の[[府内町]]3丁目、[[府内五番街商店街]]）に遷座。しかし、発展により周囲が世俗化した上、境内が手狭であったため、大正10年（[[1921年]]）に現在地に戻っている。なお、府内3丁目の旧地は、後に[[ふないアクアパーク|若竹公園]]とされ、さらに公園が移転した後には若竹ビルが建てられている。 平成15年（[[2003年]]）には、境内の一部が、新設される[[大分中央幹線道路]]（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）の予定地にかかったため、社殿を改築し境内西側に移した[http://www.oita-press.co.jp/localNews/2009_12470132922.html アツイ郷土愛 夏祭りに行こう] 大分合同新聞、2009年7月8日。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/wkmiya01.html 大分市上野町の若宮八幡社（おおいた百景）] {{DEFAULTSORT:わかみやはちまんしや}} [[Category:大分県の神社]] [[Category:八幡宮]] [[Category:大分県の旧県社]] [[Category:大分市]] {{shinto-stub}}

品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）

大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''若宮八幡社'''（わかみやはちまんしゃ）は、[[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町に鎮座する[[神社]]である。 == 概要 == 大分市中心部の南部、[[上野丘陵]]の麓に位置しており。西側で[[大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校]]に、北側で[[久大本線]]の高架線路に、南側で[[平成]]20年（[[2008年]]）に開通した[[大分中央幹線道路]]（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）に接する。 社伝では、[[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]）6月に[[大友氏]]初代当主[[大友能直]]が[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[豊後国|豊後]]二国の[[守護]]として下向した際、[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請して建立したと伝えられるが、史実は不明である。当初は古河津留（現在の岩田町周辺）に建立されたが、[[大分川]]に近く水害に遭ったため、建仁元年（[[1201年]]）に現在地の東側に遷座した。中世の[[大友氏遺跡|大友氏館]]から見て南西（[[裏鬼門]]）に位置しており、[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]の鎮守として大友氏から代々崇敬を受けた{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf 大分今昔 第十一章 若松通り]}} 渡辺克己。 [[明治]]時代に入ると、明治14年（[[1881年]]）6月に南新地（現在の[[府内町]]3丁目、[[府内五番街商店街]]）に遷座。しかし、発展により周囲が世俗化した上、境内が手狭であったため、[[大正]]10年（[[1921年]]）7月に現在地に戻っている。なお、府内3丁目の旧地は、後に[[ふないアクアパーク|若竹公園]]とされ、さらに公園が移転した後には若竹ビルが建てられている。 平成15年（[[2003年]]）には、境内の一部が、新設される大分中央幹線道路（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）の予定地にかかったため、社殿を改築し境内西側に移した[http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 アツイ郷土愛 夏祭りに行こう] 大分合同新聞、2009年7月8日。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/wkmiya01.html 大分市上野町の若宮八幡社（おおいた百景）] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:わかみやはちまんしや}} [[Category:大分県の神社]] [[Category:大分市の歴史]] [[Category:八幡宮]] [[Category:大分県の旧県社]]

大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''若宮八幡社'''（わかみやはちまんしゃ）は、[[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町に鎮座する[[神w社]]である。 == 概要 == 大分市中心部の南部、[[上野丘陵]]の麓に位置しており。西側で[[大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校]]に、北側で[[久大本線]]の高架線路に、南側で[[平成]]20年（[[2008年]]）に開通した[[大分中央幹線道路]]（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）に接する。 == 沿革 == 社伝では、[[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]）6月に[[大友氏]]初代当主[[大友能直]]が[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[豊後国|豊後]]二国の[[守護]]として下向した際、[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請して建立したと伝えられるが、史実は不明である。当初は古河津留（現在の岩田町周辺）に建立されたが、[[大分川]]に近く水害に遭ったため、建仁元年（[[1201年]]）に現在地の東側に遷座した。中世の[[大友氏遺跡|大友氏館]]から見て南西（[[裏鬼門]]）に位置しており、豊後国の国府が置かれた[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]の鎮守として大友氏から代々崇敬を受けた{{PDFlink|[http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf 大分今昔 第十一章 若松通り]}} 渡辺克己。 [[明治]]時代に入ると、明治14年（[[1881年]]）6月に南新地（現在の[[府内町]]3丁目、[[府内五番街商店街]]）に遷座。しかし、発展により周囲が世俗化した上、境内が手狭であったため、[[大正]]10年（[[1921年]]）7月に現在地に戻っている。なお、府内3丁目の旧地は、後に[[ふないアクアパーク|若竹公園]]となり、さらに公園が移転した後には若竹ビルが建てられている。 平成15年（[[2003年]]）には、境内の一部が、新設される大分中央幹線道路（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）の予定地にかかったため、社殿を改築し境内西側に移した[http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 アツイ郷土愛 夏祭りに行こう] 大分合同新聞、2009年7月8日。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [http://www.niigata-u.com/files/oita/wkmiya01.html 大分市上野町の若宮八幡社（おおいた百景）] {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:わかみやはちまんしや}} [[Category:大分県の神社]] [[Category:大分市の建築物]] [[Category:大分市の歴史]] [[Category:八幡宮]] [[Category:大分県の旧県社]]

大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''若宮八幡社'''（わかみやはちまんしゃ）は、[[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町に鎮座する[[神社]]である。 == 概要 == 大分市中心部の南部にある[[上野丘陵]]の麓に位置しており、南側で[[平成]]20年（[[2008年]]）に開通した[[大分中央幹線道路]]（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）に面している。また、西側で[[大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校]]に、北側で[[久大本線]]の高架線路に接する。 == 沿革 == 社伝では、[[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]）6月に[[大友氏]]初代当主[[大友能直]]が[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[豊後国|豊後]]二国の[[守護]]として下向した際、[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請して建立したと伝えられるが、史実は不明である。当初は古河津留（現在の岩田町周辺）に建立されたが、[[大分川]]に近く水害に遭ったため、[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）に現在地の東側に[[遷宮|遷座]]した。天正9年（[[1581年]]）から天正14年（[[1586年]]）頃の府内を描いたとされる『府内古図』{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}にも、現在地とほぼ同じ位置にこの神社が描かれている{{cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=大分市歴史的風致維持向上計画 |chapter=第2章 新旧府内の祭礼にみる歴史的風致(その2) |page=108 |publisher=大分市 |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13 }}。 [[中世]]の[[大友氏遺跡|大友氏館]]から見て南西（[[裏鬼門]]）に位置しており、豊後国の国府が置かれた[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]の鎮守として大友氏から代々崇敬を受けた{{cite web |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=大分今昔 第十一章 若松通り |author=渡辺克己 |publisher=大分合同新聞社 |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13 }}。第19代当主の[[大友義長]]は社殿を再建し、第20代当主[[大友義鑑|義鑑]]や第21代当主[[大友義鎮|義鎮]]（宗麟）は修理を行っている。また、祭礼の際には大友氏館から飾り馬を出して行幸に参加した。 [[明治]]時代に入ると、明治14年（[[1881年]]）6月に南新地（現在の[[府内町]]3丁目、[[府内五番街商店街]]）に遷座。しかし、発展により周囲が[[世俗]]化した上、[[境内]]が手狭であったため、[[大正]]10年（[[1921年]]）7月に現在地に戻っている。なお、府内3丁目の旧地は後に[[ふないアクアパーク|若竹公園]]となり、さらに公園が移転した後には若竹ビルが建てられている。 平成15年（[[2003年]]）には、境内の一部が、新設される大分中央幹線道路（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）の予定地にかかったため、社殿を改築し境内西側に移した{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=アツイ郷土愛 夏祭りに行こう |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2009-07-08 }}。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 若宮八幡社] 一般社団法人 大分市観光協会 * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ 若宮八幡社夏祭り] ツーリズムおおいた {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:わかみやはちまんしや}} [[Category:大分県の神社]] [[Category:大分市の建築物]] [[Category:大分市の歴史]] [[Category:八幡宮]] [[Category:大分県の旧県社]]

第十一章 若松通り |author=渡辺克己 |publisher=大分合同新聞社 |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13 }}。第19代当主の[[大友義長]]は社殿を再建し、第20代当主[[大友義鑑|義鑑]]や第21代当主[[大友義鎮|義鎮]]（宗麟）は修理を行っている。また、祭礼の際には大友氏館から飾り馬を出して行幸に参加した。 [[明治]]時代に入ると、明治14年（[[1881年]]）6月に南新地（現在の[[府内町]]3丁目、[[府内五番街商店街]]）に遷座。しかし、発展により周囲が[[世俗]]化した上、[[境内]]が手狭であったため、[[大正]]10年（[[1921年]]）7月に現在地に戻っている。なお、府内3丁目の旧地は後に[[ふないアクアパーク|若竹公園]]となり、さらに公園が移転した後には若竹ビルが建てられている。 平成15年（[[2003年]]）には、境内の一部が、新設される大分中央幹線道路（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）の予定地にかかったため、社殿を改築し境内西側に移した{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=アツイ郷土愛 夏祭りに行こう |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2009-07-08 }}。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 若宮八幡社] 一般社団法人 大分市観光協会 * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ 若宮八幡社夏祭り] ツーリズムおおいた {{shinto-stub}} revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 若宮八幡社 |画像 = [[File:Otita wakamiya hachimangu.jpg|280px]] |所在地 = [[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町10-28 |位置 = |祭神 = 帯中津比古命（[[仲哀天皇]]）
品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）
大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''若宮八幡社'''（わかみやはちまんしゃ）は、[[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町に鎮座する[[神社]]である。 == 概要 == 大分市中心部の南部にある[[上野丘陵]]の麓に位置しており、南側で[[平成]]20年（[[2008年]]）に開通した[[大分中央幹線道路]]（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）に面している。また、西側で[[大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校]]に、北側で[[久大本線]]の高架線路に接する。 == 沿革 == 社伝では、[[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]）6月に[[大友氏]]初代当主[[大友能直]]が[[豊前国|豊前]]・[[豊後国|豊後]]二国の[[守護]]として下向した際、[[鶴岡八幡宮]]の分霊を勧請して建立したと伝えられるが、史実は不明である。当初は古河津留（現在の岩田町周辺）に建立されたが、[[大分川]]に近く水害に遭ったため、[[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）に現在地の東側に[[遷宮|遷座]]した。天正9年（[[1581年]]）から天正14年（[[1586年]]）頃の府内を描いたとされる『府内古図』{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}にも、現在地とほぼ同じ位置にこの神社が描かれている{{cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=大分市歴史的風致維持向上計画 |chapter=第2章 新旧府内の祭礼にみる歴史的風致(その2) |page=108 |publisher=大分市 |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13 }}。 [[中世]]の[[大友氏遺跡|大友氏館]]から見て南西（[[裏鬼門]]）に位置しており、豊後国の国府が置かれた[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]の鎮守として大友氏から代々崇敬を受けた{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=大分今昔 第十一章 若松通り |author=渡辺克己 |publisher=大分合同新聞社 |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13 }}。第19代当主の[[大友義長]]は社殿を再建し、第20代当主[[大友義鑑|義鑑]]や第21代当主[[大友義鎮|義鎮]]（宗麟）は修理を行っている。また、祭礼の際には大友氏館から飾り馬を出して行幸に参加した。 [[明治]]時代に入ると、明治14年（[[1881年]]）6月に南新地（現在の[[府内町]]3丁目、[[府内五番街商店街]]）に遷座。しかし、発展により周囲が[[世俗]]化した上、[[境内]]が手狭であったため、[[大正]]10年（[[1921年]]）7月に現在地に戻っている。なお、府内3丁目の旧地は後に[[ふないアクアパーク|若竹公園]]となり、さらに公園が移転した後には若竹ビルが建てられている。 平成15年（[[2003年]]）には、境内の一部が、新設される大分中央幹線道路（都市計画道路庄の原佐野線）の予定地にかかったため、社殿を改築し境内西側に移した{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=アツイ郷土愛 夏祭りに行こう |newspaper=大分合同新聞 |date=2009-07-08 }}。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 若宮八幡社] 一般社団法人 大分市観光協会 * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ 若宮八幡社夏祭り] ツーリズムおおいた {{shinto-stub}} 品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）

report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=大分市歴史的風致維持向上計画 |chapter=第2章 新旧府内の祭礼にみる歴史的風致(その2) |page=108 |publisher=大分市 |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13 }}。 [[中世]]の[[大友氏遺跡|大友氏館]]から見て南西（[[裏鬼門]]）に位置しており、豊後国の国府が置かれた[[府内 (豊後国)|府内]]の鎮守として大友氏から代々崇敬を受けた{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=大分今昔 第十一章 若松通り |author=渡辺克己 |publisher=大分合同新聞社 |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13 }}。第1８代当主[[大友親治]]による造替、第19代当主[[大友義長]]は社殿を再建し、第20代当主[[大友義鑑|義鑑]]や第21代当主[[大友義鎮|義鎮]]（宗麟）は修理を行っている。また、祭礼の際には大友氏館から飾り馬を出して行幸に参加した。 }}。 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 若宮八幡社] 一般社団法人 大分市観光協会 * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ 若宮八幡社夏祭り] ツーリズムおおいた {{shinto-stub}} It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior Hig..."
revision.text:
```plaintext '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a {{ill|shrine|en|神社}} located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|en|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|en|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|en|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|en|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|en|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|en|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|en|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|en|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|en|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|en|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|en|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|en|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|en|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|en|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|en|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|en|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|en|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|en|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|en|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita
{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Former prefectural shrines]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Chūai]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Ōjin]] ```
[[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] ```

The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita

The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}} located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

revision.text:
'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}} located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}} located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a {{ill|shrine|ja|神社}} located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita

According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>

The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] to [[Category:defautsort]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.

According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>

The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>

revision.text:
'''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県}}.
== Overview ==
The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north.
== History ==
According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年}}).<ref>{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}}</ref>
Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.<ref name="sumiyoshi">{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.<ref name="konjaku">{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}}</ref>
In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.<ref name="sumiyoshi" /><ref>{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}}</ref>
== References == {{reflist}}
== External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita
