品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）
大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita City|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}}. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2008|qq=}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1196|qq=}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasunao|qq=}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo clan|qq=}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|qq=}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buzen Province|qq=}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo Province|qq=}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo clan|qq=}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Ura Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Chūai]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Ōjin]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[en:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]]

The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Ōjin]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[en:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building |名称 = 若宮八幡社 |image = [[File:Otita wakamiya hachimangu.jpg|280px]] |所在地 = [[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町10-28 |位置 = |祭神 = 帯中津比古命（[[仲哀天皇]]）
品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）
大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, {{ill|Ōita City|ja|大分市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita City|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōita Prefecture|ja|大分県|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Prefecture|qq=}}. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2008|qq=}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1196|qq=}} when {{ill|Ōtomo no Yasunao|ja|大友能直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Yasunao|qq=}}, the first head of the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo clan|qq=}}, brought a divided spirit of {{ill|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|ja|鶴岡八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|qq=}} to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of {{ill|Buzen Province|ja|豊前国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buzen Province|qq=}} and {{ill|Bungo Province|ja|豊後国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bungo Province|qq=}}. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the {{ill|Ōtomo clan|ja|大友氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo clan|qq=}}, who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the {{ill|Meiji period|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji period|qq=}}, the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15th year|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[en:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]]

Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[en:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]]

Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[en:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:仁徳天皇]] [[Category:大分市の建築物]] [[Category:大分市の歴史]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]]

It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1196|qq=}} when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[en:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:仁徳天皇]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:大友氏]]

It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1196|qq=}} when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Ōita City]] [[Category:History of Ōita City]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku�]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]]

大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2008|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 2008年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1196|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 建仁}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1881年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1921年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 2003年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Ōtomo clan]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:kkk]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]]

大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in {{ill|2008|ja|平成20年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2008|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 2008年}}. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in {{ill|1196|ja|建久7年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1196|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in {{ill|1201|ja|建仁元年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1201|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 建仁}} due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa {{ill|1581|ja|天正9年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1581|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to {{ill|1586|ja|天正14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1586|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1881年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1921年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 2003年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]]

It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in [[:en:1196|1196]] when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in [[:en:1201|1201]] due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in {{ill|1881|ja|明治14年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1881|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1881年}}. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in {{ill|1921|ja|大正10年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1921|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 1921年}}. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In {{ill|2003|ja|平成15年|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2003|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 2003年}}, part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]]

The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building |名称 = 若宮八幡社 |image = [[File:Otita wakamiya hachimangu.jpg|280px]] |所在地 = [[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町10-28 |位置 = |祭神 = 帯中津比古命（[[仲哀天皇]]）
品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）
大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in [[:en:2008|2008]]. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in [[:en:1196|1196]] when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in [[:en:1201|1201]] due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals in Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]]

大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = |prefecture = {{ill|Oita|qid=Q199609}} |coordinates = {{Coord|33.226887|N|131.614594|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Hachiman-zukuri|qid=Q3125455}} }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in [[:en:2008|2008]]. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in [[:en:1196|1196]] when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in [[:en:1201|1201]] due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals of Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]]

The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮|1=Ōtomo Sōrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185032}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals of Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[sn:akamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Wakamiya Hachiman-sha (Ōita City)]][[Category:qq]]

Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮|1=Ōtomo Sōrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185032}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals of Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] {{wikidata link|Q11616256}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11616256]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building |名称 = 若宮八幡社 |image = File:Otita wakamiya hachimangu.jpg |所在地 = [[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町10-28 |位置 = |祭神 = 帯中津比古命（[[仲哀天皇]]）
品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）
大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = |prefecture = {{ill|Oita|qid=Q199609}} |coordinates = {{Coord|33.226887|N|131.614594|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Hachiman-zukuri|qid=Q3125455}} }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in [[:en:2008|2008]]. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=|1=Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432699}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in [[:en:1196|1196]] when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in [[:en:1201|1201]] due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮|1=Ōtomo Sōrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185032}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals of Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] [[Category:pages without english wikidata name]] [[Category:Qq 4]] {{wikidata link|Q11616256}}

The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] {{wikidata link|Q11616256}}

[[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11616256]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:20, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building |名称 = 若宮八幡社 |image = File:Otita wakamiya hachimangu.jpg |所在地 = [[大分県]][[大分市]]上野町10-28 |位置 = |祭神 = 帯中津比古命（[[仲哀天皇]]）
品陀和気命（[[応神天皇]]）
息長帯比賣命（[[神功皇后]]）
大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = |prefecture = {{ill|Oita|WD=Q199609}} |coordinates = {{Coord|33.226887|N|131.614594|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Hachiman-zukuri|WD=Q3125455}} }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in [[:en:2008|2008]]. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|qq=|1=Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432699}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in [[:en:1196|1196]] when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in [[:en:1201|1201]] due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮|1=Ōtomo Sōrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185032}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals of Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] {{wikidata link|Q11616256}}

大雀命（[[仁徳天皇]]） |社格 = 旧県社 |創建 = [[建久]]7年（[[1196年]]） |本殿 = [[八幡造]] |別名 = 若宮様 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[7月15日]]（夏季大祭） |神事 = |prefecture = {{ill|Oita|WD=Q199609}} |coordinates = {{Coord|33.226887|N|131.614594|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |architecture_style = {{ill|Hachiman-zukuri|WD=Q3125455}} }} '''Wakamiya Hachimangū''' (若宮八幡社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Uenomachi, [[:en:Ōita (city)|Ōita City]], [[:en:Ōita Prefecture|Ōita Prefecture]]. == Overview == The shrine is situated at the base of the Ueno Hills in the southern part of Ōita City, facing the Ōita Central Main Road (an urban planned road named Shōnohara-Sano Line) that opened in [[:en:2008|2008]]. It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|1=Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432699}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in [[:en:1196|1196]] when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in [[:en:1201|1201]] due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮|1=Ōtomo Sōrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185032}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals of Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] {{wikidata link|Q11616256}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It was situated in the southwest ({{ill|Kimon|ja|裏鬼門|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ura Kimon|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 鬼門}}, the "back demon gate") relative to the clan's residence. Successive clan heads, including {{ill|Ōtomo Sōrin|ja|大友宗麟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo Sōrin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大友義鎮|1=Ōtomo Sōrin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1185032}}, funded its reconstruction and maintenance.{{Cite report|和書 |url=https://www.city.oita.oita.jp/o169/documents/sumiyoshijinnjyanosaireikarashinnkyuufunaimatome.pdf |title=Ōita City Historical Landscape Conservation Plan |chapter=Chapter 2: Festivals of Old and New Funai |page=108 |publisher=Ōita City |date=2019-06 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} During the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]], the shrine was moved to Minamishinchi (present-day Funaimachi 3-chome) in [[:en:1881|1881]]. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] {{wikidata link|Q11616256}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

It borders the {{ill|Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|ja|大分市立上野ヶ丘中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|1=Ōita Municipal Uenogaoka Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11432699}} to the west and the elevated tracks of the Kyūdai Main Line to the north. == History == According to the shrine's tradition, it was established in [[:en:1196|1196]] when [[:en:Ōtomo Yoshinao|Ōtomo no Yasunao]], the first head of the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], brought a divided spirit of [[:en:Tsurugaoka Hachimangū|Tsurugaoka Hachimangū]] to the region while serving as the governor of the provinces of [[:en:Buzen Province|Buzen Province]] and [[:en:Bungo Province|Bungo Province]]. Initially located in Furukawazuru (present-day Iwata-chō), the shrine was moved to its current eastern site in [[:en:1201|1201]] due to repeated flooding from the Ōita River. The shrine's location is depicted in the "Funaikozu," a map of Funai from the Tenshō period (circa [[:en:1581|1581]] to [[:en:1586|1586]]).{{文化遺産オンライン|95170|府内古図}} Historically, the shrine was revered by the [[:en:Ōtomo clan|Ōtomo clan]], who regarded it as a guardian of their castle in Funai. However, due to urbanization and space constraints, it was relocated back to its current site in [[:en:1921|1921]]. The former site later became Wakadake Park and was eventually replaced by the Wakadake Building.{{Cite web|和書 |url=http://www.nan-nan.jp/lib/oita011a1.pdf |title=Ōita Then and Now: Chapter 11 - Wakamatsu Street |author=Katsumi Watanabe |publisher=Ōita Godō Shimbunsha |date=2007-10-26 |accessdate=2020-01-13}} In [[:en:2003|2003]], part of the shrine's grounds was used for the construction of the Ōita Central Main Road. The shrine buildings were renovated and moved to the western section of the grounds.{{cite news |url=http://www.oitatv.com/chiiki/index.php?id=1078 |title=Passionate Local Spirit: Let's Visit Summer Festivals |newspaper=Ōita Godō Shimbun |date=2009-07-08}} == References == {{reflist}} == External Links == * [https://www.oishiimati-oita.jp/spots/detail/733 Wakamiya Hachimangū] - Ōita City Tourism Association * [https://www.visit-oita.jp/events/detail/11467/ Wakamiya Hachimangū Summer Festival] - Tourism Ōita {{Shinto-stub}} {{translated page|ja|若宮八幡社 (大分市)|version=100315887|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 6]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] }} [[ja:若宮八幡社 (大分市)]] [[Category:Emperor Nintoku]] [[Category:Buildings in Oita, Oita]] [[Category:History of Oita, Oita]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Oita Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ōita Prefecture]] [[Category:Bungo Otomo clan]] {{wikidata link|Q11616256}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]
